summary: Fowler explores the design challenge of modeling currency in software systems. While currency typically works well as a simple value object using just the currency code, he describes a system that required mutable 'pip values' for UI nudge buttons. Since value objects should be immutable, the solution involved splitting currency into two classes: a value object for immutable data like the currency code, and a reference object for mutable data like pip values. This 'bi-semantic' pattern is rare, and most systems can use simple value objects for currency.
key_points: Currency typically works well as a simple value object containing just the currency code
Value objects should be immutable, which becomes problematic when some currency attributes need to change
The solution was splitting into two classes: a value object for immutable data and a reference object for mutable data
The value object delegated certain methods (like pip value) to the reference object held in a lookup table
This bi-semantic pattern is unusual and rarely needed in practice
money_quotes: For many systems currency works well as a value, the key part you need is the internationally recognized currency code.
This violates a very useful rule of value objects - that they are immutable.
The value object was passed around most of the time, but some methods in the value object, such as pip value, delegated to the reference object.
This bi-semantic behavior is unusual - it's the only time I've seen it.
key_insight: When a concept needs both immutable identity and mutable attributes, split it into a value object and a reference object that work together.
